critic_raw:
The split strategy uses 'stratified random sampling' with 'a random 15% of observations held out as the test set.' For a 36-month time-series manufacturing dataset with failure labels defined by events within a 7-day forward window, random splitting creates severe temporal leakage: a window from month 35 can be in the training set while a window from month 5 is in the test set. In manufacturing environments, equipment degrades over time, sensor patterns change as machines age, and failure modes evolve. A model trained on windows that include future time periods has access to information unavailable in real deployment.

The logistic regression baseline receives only aggregated sensor statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend) without operator log or context features. The RNN receives the full multi-modal input including operator logs. This is a fundamentally unfair comparison that conflates multimodal information advantage with architectural advantage. The design explicitly states 'the baseline does not receive operator logs or context features,' but the framing presents this as a controlled comparison. In reality, any performance advantage of the RNN could reflect the operator log signal rather than the RNN's sequential modeling capacity.

Preprocessing states 'all preprocessing steps are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.' This is correctly described and represents sound practice. However, the split strategy is random sampling without temporal ordering, so 'training set' in a random split does not correspond to chronologically earlier data — fitting preprocessing on a randomly selected 85% sample still means test-period data can influence imputation and scaling if some test-period observations are in the training sample.

The minimum recall constraint (recall ≥ 0.85) is enforced during threshold selection but the threshold is determined per-model on the test set to 'identify the probability decision threshold that satisfies recall ≥ 0.85 and maximizes F1.' Threshold selection on the test set is a form of post-hoc tuning — the threshold should be determined on the validation set and applied fixed to the test set.
all_issues_raised: Random temporal split for 36-month time-series sensor data allows training on future windows and testing on past windows, invalidating deployment-relevant evaluation
Baseline receives only sensor statistics while RNN receives full multi-modal input including operator logs — conflates information advantage with architectural advantage
Threshold selection on test set: minimum recall constraint applied by sweeping thresholds on test predictions rather than locking threshold from validation set
Context features (equipment age, days since maintenance, shift) provided to RNN but not to logistic regression baseline — further confounds the comparison
Rolling 100-step windows from the same failure episode distributed across train and test via random splitting creates near-duplicate contamination

Temporal autocorrelation leakage: random sampling of 100-minute rolling sensor windows from a continuous 36-month stream places overlapping windows in different splits, allowing test windows to be near-identical to training windows

Failure event window overlap across splits: a single failure event at time T generates 10,080 positive-labeled overlapping windows; random split distributes these across train and test, allowing the model to see partial windows of the same failure event during both training and evaluation

TF-IDF dimensionality mismatch for technical operator logs: 100-dimension TF-IDF is a poor representation for sparse domain-specific maintenance text that has strong categorical structure; direct categorical encoding with learned embeddings would be more appropriate

Operator log temporal alignment unspecified: the design does not address how operator logs (sparse, event-driven) are aligned with sensor windows (continuous, 1-minute) when logs are absent within a window—the 'missing token' strategy for absent logs conflates data absence with operational silence

No equipment-level independence in the split: if thousands of machines share the same facility infrastructure (shared coolant, power), inter-equipment correlations mean that randomly split windows from different machines may share common failure precursors that constitute leakage across equipment units
Stratified random split used for 36 months of temporally ordered sensor data, allowing future observations to appear in training and inflating performance estimates relative to prospective deployment

Logistic regression baseline does not receive operator logs or context features available to the RNN, creating a feature-set asymmetry that confounds architecture comparison with information advantage
Design claims 'the temporal split automatically accounts for temporal trends' but describes a random split — this is an internal contradiction that signals confusion about the split methodology

No patient or equipment-level assignment check: if rolling windows of 100 consecutive observations span across the random split boundary, adjacent windows from the same equipment unit can appear in both training and test sets

Minimum recall threshold of 0.85 is operationally motivated but fixed without empirical justification for this specific plant and failure type; threshold sensitivity analysis would strengthen the approach
